title: The Empire That Wouldn't Die
bullets: Nobody in this entire 1,123-year story ever called themselves a Byzantine — they called themselves Romaioi, Romans, and their state the Basileia ton Romaion, Empire of the Romans.
The word 'Byzantine' was invented by a German scholar named Hieronymus Wolf in 1557, more than a century after the empire was already gone, to label a state he wanted to distinguish from 'real' Rome.

Constantine won the Battle of the Milvian Bridge in 312, claiming a vision of the Chi-Rho symbol, and then issued the Edict of Milan in 313 legalizing Christianity across the empire after three centuries of persecution.

The First Council of Nicaea in 325 produced the Nicene Creed, settling the Arian controversy by declaring Christ 'of one substance with the Father,' a resolution that sparked fifty more years of persecution before it held.

Constantine chose Byzantion, a small Greek fishing town on the Bosporus, as his new capital in 324, renaming it Constantinople — the most defensible major-city site in the Mediterranean, with sea on three sides and only a short landward wall to seal off the west.

The Theodosian Walls, built 408–413 under the regent Anthemios, were a layered system of ditch, outer wall, terrace, and inner wall up to 40 feet high with 96 towers — not breached by a foreign army for over a thousand years.

The Council of Chalcedon in 451 defined Christ as two natures without confusion or separation, but the Egyptian and Syrian Monophysite churches rejected the verdict permanently, a schism that would cost the empire those provinces when the Arabs arrived.

When Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus in 476 and sent the western imperial regalia to Constantinople, the Byzantine court called it an administrative consolidation — the empire was again one state with one emperor.

title: Justinian's Reconquest
bullets: Justin I, an illiterate Thracian peasant who had walked to Constantinople as a pig farmer's son, became emperor in 518 because he commanded the palace guard regiment when Anastasius I died without an heir.
Justin's nephew Justinian, educated at imperial expense in law, theology, and Greek philosophy, was already running the actual administration when he formally inherited the throne in 527.

Theodora, the co-empress Justinian married after changing the law against patrician-actress marriages, halted a panicked imperial flight during the Nika riots with the line 'Royalty is a good burial-shroud,' saving the throne at a cost of 30,000 dead rioters in the Hippodrome.

The Corpus Juris Civilis, completed 529–534 under the jurist Tribonian, codified a thousand years of Roman law into four parts and became the foundational legal text of France, Germany, Spain, Italy, Louisiana, Latin America, and Japan.

Hagia Sophia, built in five years and ten months by the mathematician-architects Anthemius of Tralles and Isidore of Miletus, used the pendentive — a triangular concave surface filling the corner between two walls — to place a 102-foot circular dome on a square base for the first time at this scale.

Belisarius reconquered North Africa from the Vandals in less than a year with 15,000 men, then spent nineteen grinding years reconquering Italy from the Ostrogoths, leaving Rome itself depopulated to below 50,000 inhabitants.

The Plague of Justinian arrived in Constantinople in 541, killing an estimated quarter of the eastern Mediterranean's population in waves over two centuries, permanently collapsing tax revenues and the army's recruiting base.

Two Nestorian monks smuggled silkworm eggs out of China in hollow bamboo walking sticks in 552, breaking the Chinese silk monopoly and founding a Byzantine state-controlled industry that lasted as long as the empire did.
Justinian died in 565 having reconquered North Africa, Italy, and southern Spain, codified the law, and built Hagia Sophia — and left his successor an empty treasury.

title: The Heraclian Convulsion
bullets: Maurice, the most capable emperor of the post-Justinian generation, was seized and executed with his five sons in 602 when the Danube army mutinied over a cut in pay, starting a pattern of coup instability that lasted four hundred years.

Phocas's disaster presidency let the Sasanians cross the Euphrates in 603 and take Antioch (611), Damascus (613), Jerusalem (614 — carrying off the True Cross), and Egypt (619), reducing the empire to Anatolia and the Balkans.

Heraclius, sailing from Carthage to overthrow Phocas in 610, inherited an empire that by any objective accounting was finished — and then switched the empire's official language from Latin to Greek, the most consequential administrative decision of his reign.

Heraclius counterattacked the Persians in 622, maneuvering through the Caucasus and Azerbaijan for six years, burning the great Zoroastrian fire-temple at Adur Gushnasp, and decisively routing the main Sasanian army at the Battle of Nineveh on December 12, 627.

In March 630, Heraclius rode into Jerusalem barefoot carrying the recovered True Cross — the high point of his reign — while both Byzantium and Sasanian Persia stood exhausted, bankrupt, and depopulated.

The Arab army under Khalid ibn al-Walid destroyed the main Roman field force at the Battle of Yarmouk in August 636, exploiting a sandstorm; within two years Damascus, Antioch, and Jerusalem had all fallen permanently.

The Coptic and Monophysite populations of Egypt and Syria — persecuted by Constantinople for two centuries over the Chalcedonian schism — offered little resistance to the Arab takeover, which came with lower taxes and freedom of worship.

The theme system replaced the old salaried professional army with hereditary soldier-farmers granted military lands, simultaneously saving the army, giving Anatolian peasants a stake in defending their land, and producing the military aristocracy that would dominate Byzantine politics for centuries.

Maximos the Confessor, who rejected the Monothelite compromise, had his tongue and right hand cut off for speaking and writing against imperial policy; nineteen years after his death, the Sixth Ecumenical Council vindicated him and anathematized the pope who had endorsed Monothelitism.

title: The Iconoclast Century
bullets: Greek fire — probably a mixture of crude petroleum, pine resin, sulfur, and quicklime that ignited on contact with seawater and burned on the surface — destroyed the Umayyad fleet in 678 and again in 718, guarding its formula through a single family and a single imperial workshop.

The Second Arab Siege of 717–718 brought perhaps 80,000 men and 1,800 ships against Constantinople; only five ships returned to the Caliphate, and the failure ended any further Arab attempt on the city by sea.

Leo III's 726 edict removing the icon of Christ above the Chalke Gate started the iconoclast controversy by arguing that the empire's military disasters were divine punishment for the idolatrous veneration of images.

John of Damascus, writing safely under Umayyad rule beyond the Byzantine emperor's reach, developed the decisive theological distinction between veneration — honor passing through the image to its prototype — and worship, which belongs to God alone.

Constantine V convened the Council of Hieria in 754 with 338 bishops, declared it the Seventh Ecumenical Council, and had it condemn icon veneration as idolatry — a verdict Rome rejected and neither the Catholic nor Orthodox churches recognize today.

The iconoclast rupture drove Pope Stephen II to Pepin the Short for protection, and Pepin's Italian donations created the Papal States; on Christmas Day 800, Leo III crowned Charlemagne Imperator Romanorum, establishing the Holy Roman Empire as a Latin rival to Constantinople.

Empress Irene in 797 had her own son Constantine VI blinded and deposed in the room where she had given birth to him, then ruled in her own right as 'Basileus' — the masculine title — until her overthrow in 802, while the Pope used the alleged 'vacancy' of the throne to crown Charlemagne.

Theophilos, the last iconoclast emperor, commissioned the scholar Leo the Mathematician to design throne-room automata — roaring golden lions, singing mechanical birds, a throne that rose toward the ceiling — and an optical telegraph that could relay frontier alarms to Constantinople in under an hour.

The formal restoration of icon veneration on March 11, 843, celebrated ever since as the Triumph of Orthodoxy, is still the major liturgical feast of the first Sunday of Lent in every Eastern Orthodox church.

title: The Macedonian Renaissance
bullets: Basil I, a physically imposing Armenian stable groom who had risen through the palace to murder both the emperor's chief minister and then the emperor himself, founded the longest-lasting dynasty in Byzantine history.

Patriarch Photius, the most learned man of the ninth century, compiled the Bibliotheca — summaries and critiques of 279 ancient books, many now lost — and was twice embroiled in schisms with Rome that rehearsed every argument later deployed in 1054.

Cyril invented the Glagolitic script in 862 specifically to capture the sounds of Slavic, and his disciples developed the simplified Cyrillic alphabet from it — a script that became the writing system of Bulgarian, Serbian, Russian, Ukrainian, Belarusian, and dozens of other languages.

By translating the Bible and liturgy into Old Church Slavonic rather than insisting on Latin, Constantinople gave Slavic converts Christianity in their own tongue, producing an Orthodox Slavic world that extended from the Adriatic to the Volga.

Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos, pushed aside as a figurehead emperor for decades, used his forced leisure to commission the De Administrando Imperio — a confidential geopolitical manual describing every neighboring people and how to play them off against each other.

The Souda, a 30,000-entry Greek encyclopedia compiled around 980, preserves quotations and biographical details from hundreds of ancient works that have not otherwise survived; most of what we know about pre-Christian Greek civilization comes through it.

Nikephoros II Phokas reconquered Crete in 961 and Antioch in 969 — one of the original five Christian patriarchal sees, lost to the Arabs for 330 years — before being assassinated in a palace coup organized by his wife and her lover.

In 988 Vladimir of Kyiv converted to Orthodox Christianity as a condition of marrying Basil II's sister, mass-baptized his people in the Dnieper, and brought 6,000 Norse-Rus warriors as the new Varangian Guard — fanatically loyal to whoever held the throne, regardless of how they had gotten there.

Basil II ordered 15,000 Bulgarian prisoners blinded after the Battle of Kleidion in 1014 — leaving one man in every hundred with a single eye to lead the others home — and Tsar Samuel reportedly died of a stroke two days after seeing his army return.

title: Ceremony, Silk, and the Hippodrome
bullets: Constantinople in 1000 had 400,000 to 500,000 inhabitants — the largest city in Europe by an enormous margin, with Paris at perhaps 20,000 and London at 15,000.
Of the eighty-eight Byzantine emperors between 330 and 1453, roughly a third died violently — assassinated, blinded and deposed, killed in battle, or executed after a coup.

Imperial court eunuchs occupied reserved offices — parakoimomenos, protovestiarios, praepositus sacri cubiculi — because they could not father children and were legally barred from becoming emperor, structurally aligning their interests with the throne's stability rather than their own dynasty.

The Hippodrome seated perhaps 100,000 spectators and was where emperors learned what their subjects thought of them — the same space where Justinian nearly lost his throne, where Andronikos I was lynched, and where successful charioteers were more famous than most of the emperors of their century.

The Byzantine bezant, minted at 4.5 grams of nearly pure gold for over six centuries, was the international reserve currency of the Mediterranean — hoarded by rulers from Britain to India and trusted in foreign markets as a store of value wherever it appeared.

The Book of the Eparch, a tenth-century regulatory manual for Constantinople's guilds, describes who could buy raw silk, who could weave it, who could sell it, and what prices applied — a far more formalized commercial economy than anything in contemporary Western Europe.

The Divine Liturgy in Hagia Sophia took roughly two hours, surrounded by gold mosaics, hundreds of oil lamps, and clouds of incense — Byzantine writers describe the experience as walking into a vision of heaven where the boundary between earthly and divine had become thin.

Most of the Greek classical texts that survive into the modern world — Plato, Aristotle, the tragedians, the historians — reach us through Macedonian-era copies written in the new minuscule script; the Athens of Plato was preserved by the Constantinople of Constantine VII.

title: The Slow Crack
bullets: The nomisma had been minted at 4.5 grams of essentially pure gold for over six centuries; by the 1070s its gold content had fallen to around 30%, destroying its status as the Mediterranean's reserve currency.

Empress Zoe Porphyrogennita, the legitimate Macedonian heir, was 50 when she first married and went through three husbands in succession — one drowned in his bath by his successor — while briefly ruling jointly with her sister Theodora as the only time two women reigned simultaneously in Byzantine history.

Michael Psellos, the most influential intellectual of the period, wrote the Chronographia — a memoir of the emperors he served in luminous Greek prose, full of psychological observation and dry court gossip — while also reviving Platonic philosophy to an extent that worried his more orthodox contemporaries.

On July 16, 1054, Cardinal Humbert marched into Hagia Sophia in the middle of the Divine Liturgy and laid a bull of excommunication on the high altar; Patriarch Cerularius excommunicated him in return, a public and theatrical break that neither side's authority quite justified but that neither reversed.
The mutual excommunications of 1054 were lifted in 1965 by Pope Paul VI and Patriarch Athenagoras; the actual schism between Catholic and Orthodox Christianity is still in place.

At the Battle of Manzikert in 1071, the reserve corps under Andronikos Doukas deliberately withdrew from the field rather than support Emperor Romanos IV, turning the battle into a rout and producing the first Roman emperor captured alive since Valerian eight centuries earlier.

Alp Arslan released Romanos IV rather than killing him, calculating that a returning emperor whose authority was contested would weaken Byzantium more than a power vacuum — a calculation that proved correct when the Doukas faction deposed and blinded Romanos on his way home.

Within a decade of Manzikert, the Sultanate of Rum had been established at Iznik — the old Nicaea — a hundred miles from Constantinople, converting Anatolia from a Greek-speaking Christian farming region into a Turkish-speaking Muslim one over the next two centuries.

title: The Komnenian Restoration
bullets: Alexios I Komnenos took the throne in 1081 at age 24, in a coup so fast that his own troops celebrated by sacking the wealthy quarters of Constantinople for three days.

The Chrysobull of 1082, issued to buy Venetian naval help against Norman invasion, gave Venice sweeping exemptions from Byzantine customs duties — a deal that gradually handed Italian merchants control of Byzantine long-distance commerce and that one historian has called the death warrant of Byzantine commercial independence.

Pope Urban II transformed Alexios's request for western mercenaries into a call for a general crusade at Clermont in November 1095, producing armies far larger than anything Alexios had imagined and far less controllable than anything he had wanted.

Anna Komnene, born in the imperial purple chamber in 1083, wrote the Alexiad — a history of her father's reign and the First Crusade — the first major historical work known to have been written by a woman in the European tradition.

The crusaders swore oaths to Alexios to return conquered former Byzantine territory but kept Antioch for Bohemond of Taranto; Alexios considered their betrayal a permanent grievance, the crusaders considered his failure to relieve them at Antioch a permanent grievance, and both sides spent a century nursing both.

John II Komnenos destroyed Pecheneg power forever at the Battle of Beroia in 1122 and died in 1143 from a poisoned arrow wound inflicted accidentally by his own quiver while hunting boar — an accident so improbable that contemporaries suspected assassination.

Manuel I's defeat at the Battle of Myriokephalon in 1176, when his massive expedition to retake Anatolia was ambushed in a mountain pass, ended Byzantine hopes of recovering the Anatolian interior; after Myriokephalon, Anatolia was permanently Turkic.

Andronikos I Komnenos, after a lifetime of adventures from Constantinople to Georgia to the wife of the Prince of Antioch, seized the throne at around age 65 and was lynched in the Hippodrome three years later — his eyes gouged out, his right hand cut off, his teeth pulled — by the same crowd that had cheered his coup.
The Latin massacre of 1182, in which Constantinople's population killed or enslaved perhaps 60,000 Italian merchants over several days, was a direct cause of the Fourth Crusade twenty years later.

title: The Catastrophe of 1204
bullets: In April 1204, western Christian crusaders stormed the largest Christian city in the world, sacked it for three days, and destroyed more of the surviving heritage of classical antiquity than any single event between the Library of Alexandria and the Reformation.

The Fourth Crusade began as a campaign to liberate Jerusalem, was redirected to attack the Catholic city of Zara to pay Venetian transport debts, and then was diverted to Constantinople on the promise of a deposed Byzantine prince who had no resources to deliver what he offered.

The 90-year-old blind doge Enrico Dandolo personally led the Venetian assault on the sea walls of Constantinople in April 1204, according to his own biographers — a practical illustration of his role in engineering the entire diversion.

Niketas Choniates, a Greek bishop and historian who was an eyewitness, recorded in detail the looting of Hagia Sophia, the opening of imperial tombs, the melting of bronze monuments for coinage, and a prostitute placed on the Patriarch's throne to sing obscene songs.

The four bronze horses of the Hippodrome were carted to Venice in 1204; the originals now stand inside the Basilica of San Marco, with replicas visible from the square — they have been in Venice for over eight hundred years.

John III Doukas Vatatzes, the most successful ruler of the Nicaean exile state, reportedly issued chickens and grain to peasant villages to encourage food production while rebuilding the army, the Patriarchate, and the diplomatic infrastructure of a legitimate Byzantine government.

On July 25, 1261, the Nicaean general Alexios Strategopoulos discovered through local contacts that Constantinople's Latin garrison had been evacuated to fight elsewhere, led his 800-man reconnaissance force through a postern gate at night, and took the city without significant resistance.

Constantinople in 1261 had perhaps 35,000 inhabitants, down from at least 400,000 before 1204; whole neighborhoods were depopulated, aqueducts had been sabotaged, and Hagia Sophia was a stripped Catholic cathedral missing its gold mosaics, silver iconostasis, and liturgical vessels.

title: Palaiologan Twilight
bullets: Andronikos II dismantled the Byzantine navy to save money and contracted maritime defense to the Genoese, who used their position to establish a fortified enclave at Galata across the Golden Horn with its own laws, taxes, and fleet outside Byzantine sovereignty.

The Catalan Company, hired in 1303 to fight the Turks, was spectacularly effective in battle and impossible to control; after their commander Roger de Flor was assassinated at a banquet, the Catalans sacked Thrace and Macedonia for four years, then seized the Duchy of Athens and ruled it for seventy-seven years.

The Byzantine Civil War of 1341–1347 was simultaneously a dynastic dispute, a class conflict, and a theological dispute; John Kantakouzenos invited Ottoman mercenaries across to fight on his behalf, setting the precedent that brought them permanently to Europe.

Kantakouzenos married his daughter Theodora to the Ottoman emir Orhan as part of a military alliance; the Ottoman forces he called back in 1352 occupied the earthquake-ruined fortress at Gallipoli and did not fully leave, securing a permanent European bridgehead.

The Black Death killed between a third and half of Constantinople's remaining population in 1347 — the worst demographic catastrophe in Byzantine history — and kept recurring for decades, suppressing any population recovery.

Gregory Palamas, the Mount Athos monk who became Archbishop of Thessalonica, developed the distinction between God's unknowable essence and his knowable uncreated energies — the theological foundation of Hesychasm — which became official Eastern Orthodox doctrine at the councils of 1341, 1347, and 1351.

The fall of Nicaea to Sultan Orhan in 1331 — city of the First and Second Ecumenical Councils, wartime capital of Byzantium from 1204 to 1261 — was a profound symbolic blow; Andronikos III had already failed to relieve it at the Battle of Pelekanon in 1329.

The Ottoman victory at Kosovo in 1389 eliminated the Serbs as a potential Byzantine ally; by that point the Byzantine emperor had already become a formal Ottoman vassal, required to pay tribute, provide troops, and appear at the sultan's court when summoned.

Tamerlane's defeat of Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara on July 28, 1402 — capturing the Ottoman ruler who had been preparing to besiege Constantinople — was an entirely external gift that bought the empire a fifty-year reprieve it had done nothing to earn.

title: The Last Hundred Years and the Fall
bullets: Emperor Manuel II spent Christmas 1400 at the court of Henry IV in London — the Roman emperor traveling as a supplicant through former Roman provinces whose rulers he had to flatter for favors — and returned having extracted no concrete military aid.

The Council of Florence (1438–1445) produced a formal Decree of Union on July 6, 1439, with Greek bishops accepting papal supremacy and the Filioque; Mark of Ephesus refused to sign, and the Greek population refused to accept the result when the delegation returned home.

The attributed quip of grand admiral Loukas Notaras — 'Better the Sultan's turban in the city than the Cardinal's mitre' — captured popular sentiment; the Union collapsed as dead letter within a few years without delivering the promised western military aid.

The crusade Pope Eugene IV organized to fulfill Florence's promises was destroyed at the Battle of Varna on November 10, 1444, killing the Polish-Hungarian king Vladislav III and ending any prospect of large-scale European intervention against the Ottomans.

Mehmed II built Rumeli Hisarı in 1452 — a fortress sealing the Bosporus between the Black Sea and Constantinople — and commissioned the Hungarian engineer Orban, who had first approached the Byzantines and been told they could not pay his price, to cast cannons firing 1,200-pound stone balls.

Giovanni Giustiniani Longo, a Genoese soldier, brought his own troops to Constantinople at his own expense, volunteering to fight in what he correctly understood as the great Christian cause of the age; his wounding during the final assault directly caused the Greek defensive collapse.

Mehmed dragged seventy ships overland on a greased wooden ramp over the hill behind Galata on the night of April 22, bypassing the Golden Horn chain and adding a new front to the siege that forced the defenders to spread their thin manpower even thinner.

Constantine XI threw off his imperial robes and rushed into the press of Ottoman troops at the breach on the morning of May 29, 1453; his body was never definitely identified, and he remains the only Byzantine emperor whose death was witnessed by no surviving contemporary.

Mehmed cut the three days of customary plunder to one day on the evening of May 29 to preserve as much of his new capital as possible; the first Islamic prayers in Hagia Sophia were performed on Friday, June 1, 1453.

title: The Inheritance
bullets: Byzantine scholars — including Bessarion, who donated over six hundred manuscripts to Venice to found the Biblioteca Marciana — brought the Greek originals of Plato, Aristotle, Sophocles, Thucydides, and the major Greek texts to Italian humanists who had previously known them only in fragmentary Latin paraphrases.

Gemistos Plethon, attending the Council of Florence as an elderly court philosopher, gave lectures on Plato that so impressed Cosimo de' Medici that Cosimo founded the Florentine Platonic Academy under Marsilio Ficino, which translated Plato's complete works into Latin for the first time.

Mehmed II appointed the anti-unionist George Scholarios as Patriarch Gennadios II and granted the Greek Orthodox church civil authority over Christian populations under the Rum millet — recognizing that a stable Greek church served his interests better than a leaderless one.

The Patriarchate of Constantinople continued functioning under Ottoman administration for the next four centuries, serving as the institutional vehicle through which Greek Orthodox identity, language, and culture survived until the Greek War of Independence in 1821.

Zoe Palaiologina, a Byzantine princess who traveled north to marry Grand Duke Ivan III of Moscow in 1472, brought the Byzantine double-headed eagle as the symbol of the Russian state — still on the Russian flag today.

The doctrine of Moscow as the Third Rome, formulated around 1510, argued that the first Rome had fallen to barbarians, the second to Turks and the heresy of Florence, and that Moscow was the last surviving seat of true Orthodox Christianity with no fourth to follow.

Justinian's Corpus Juris Civilis, rediscovered at Bologna around 1070 and taught at every major European university thereafter, became the foundational text for the legal codes of France, Germany, Spain, Italy, the Netherlands, Quebec, Louisiana, Latin America, and Japan.

Mimar Sinan's Süleymaniye Mosque (1557) and Selimiye Mosque (1574) are direct descendants of Hagia Sophia's pendentive dome — not just Hagia Sophia's children, but arguably its best children, structurally more sophisticated and more elegantly proportioned.
Roughly a quarter of a billion Christians today belong to Orthodox churches that trace their religious inheritance, liturgical practice, theology, and iconography directly to medieval Constantinople.
